# ETF liquidity regime triage workflow

Source: Finance Method Notebook
Publish Date: 2026-04-27
Extraction Summary: This article extracts a bounded finance research workflow for triaging ETF event risk through liquidity, funding, and credit-regime evidence before any research conclusion.
Capability Name: ETF liquidity regime triage workflow
Capability Type: analysis_method
Related Finance Domains: etf_regime, credit_liquidity
Capability Tags: event_catalyst_mapping, risk_gate_design
Method Summary: Translate ETF event headlines into liquidity-regime review buckets, separating funding stress from ordinary headline volatility.
Required Data Sources: ETF issuer notes, credit spreads, funding stress indicators, public headlines
Causal Claim: ETF event risk can become more decision-relevant when public headlines align with funding stress and credit-spread pressure, but the alignment does not prove a trading edge by itself.
Evidence Categories: equity_market_evidence, etf_regime_evidence, credit_evidence, liquidity_evidence, event_catalyst_evidence, portfolio_risk_evidence, implementation_evidence
Evidence Summary: ETF issuer notes, credit-spread context, funding-stress indicators, and public headlines support a bounded liquidity-regime triage review.
Evidence Level: case_study
Implementation Requirements: Maintain a dated liquidity checklist, compare event clusters with credit and funding indicators, and refuse timing language unless fresh inputs confirm the regime link.
Risk and Failure Modes: Liquidity stress can normalize quickly, credit spreads can lag equity moves, and event headlines can exaggerate risk during short-lived volatility spikes.
Overfitting or Spurious Risk: A few crisis-like examples can make liquidity overlays look more predictive than they are across normal ETF regimes.
Compliance or Collection Notes: Use public articles, local exports, issuer materials, and manually captured data only.
Suggested Attachment Point: research_capability:risk_gate_design
Allowed Action Authority: research_only

This note describes a reusable research method only and requires later human review before any market conclusion.
